## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**
**"Differentiate between a 'conflict' and a 'dispute' in the context of construction contracts. Illustrate with a suitable example." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **conflict** is a latent, underlying condition of disagreement between parties that has not yet been formally articulated. It exists as a potential for disagreement, often arising from differences in expectations, values, or interests. A **dispute**, on the other hand, is a crystallized, formal claim made by one party against another, asserting a right, alleging a breach, or seeking a specific remedy.

*Example:* In a highway construction project, a contractor believing that the employer is deliberately delaying the issuance of drawings represents a **conflict** (latent tension). The moment the contractor sends a written notice under FIDIC Sub-Clause 20.1 claiming extension of time and additional cost, the conflict has crystallized into a **dispute** — actionable, quantifiable, and subject to formal resolution.

The distinction is critical because the contractual machinery (tiered dispute resolution clause) is triggered only when a **dispute** arises, not by the mere existence of a **conflict**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — December 2023 Model Question]**
**"State the essential ingredients of a valid arbitration agreement under Section 7 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Under **Section 7** of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, a valid arbitration agreement must satisfy the following essentials:

1. **In Writing:** The agreement must be in writing, contained in a document signed by the parties, or in an exchange of letters, telex, telegram, or electronic communication, or in a document exchanged between the parties that incorporates an arbitration clause by reference.
2. **Defined Legal Relationship:** The agreement must relate to a defined legal relationship, whether contractual or non-contractual.
3. **Disputes Covered:** It must cover disputes that have arisen or which may arise between the parties.
4. **Parties' Capacity:** Both parties must have legal capacity to enter into a contract.
5. **Arbitrability:** The subject matter of the dispute must be arbitrable.
6. **No Vitiating Factors:** Consent must be free from fraud, coercion, misrepresentation, or undue influence.

The agreement may take the form of an *arbitration clause* within a contract or a *separate submission agreement* (Section 7(2)).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question — Module 4 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the various **causes of conflicts and disputes** in construction contracts. Categorize them into contractual, behavioural, project-execution, external, and financial causes with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe **tiered dispute resolution clauses**. Draw a typical 4-tier framework with time limits and the legal nature of each tier. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Causes of Conflicts and Disputes:**

Conflicts and disputes in construction contracts arise from a multiplicity of factors. They can be categorized as follows:

1. **Contractual Causes (3 sub-causes):**
   - *Ambiguous clauses* in the contract (e.g., unclear scope of work) lead to differing interpretations.
   - *Incomplete scope definitions* leave room for variations and claims.
   - *Mismatched risk allocation* (e.g., imposing unforeseeable ground conditions on the contractor) creates adversarial dynamics.

2. **Behavioural/Relational Causes (3 sub-causes):**
   - *Lack of communication* between site engineers and the project manager escalates minor issues.
   - *Adversarial culture* inherited from past projects leads to claim-counterclaim cycles.
   - *Trust deficit* — for example, the employer suspects the contractor of over-claiming, and vice versa.

3. **Project-Execution Causes (3 sub-causes):**
   - *Delays* due to late drawings, material shortages, or labor disputes.
   - *Scope creep* — additional works added without proper change-order mechanism.
   - *Design errors and unforeseen ground conditions* trigger variation claims.

4. **External Causes (2 sub-causes):**
   - *Regulatory changes* (e.g., new environmental clearance rules).
   - *Force majeure* events (cyclones, pandemics, war).

5. **Financial Causes (2 sub-causes):**
   - *Payment delays* by the employer create cash-flow crises for the contractor.
   - *Currency fluctuations* in international projects alter the contract value mid-execution.

*(Examiner's Note: Each category should be illustrated with at least one real-world example from infrastructure projects in Kerala — e.g., GIFT, KIIFB-funded road projects, metro rail, etc.)*

**Part (b) — Tiered Dispute Resolution Clauses:**

A **tiered dispute resolution clause** (also called Multi-Tiered Dispute Resolution or MTDR clause) is a contractual provision that mandates parties to progress sequentially through a hierarchy of dispute resolution mechanisms before any dispute can be escalated to formal binding arbitration or litigation.

**The 4-Tier Framework:**

| Tier | Method | Typical Duration | Nature | Outcome |
|------|--------|------------------|--------|---------|
| **1** | Direct Negotiation between Senior Executives | 14–30 days | Consensual, amicable | Settlement Agreement (binding) |
| **2** | Mediation with a Neutral Mediator | 30–60 days | Consensual, facilitative | Settlement Agreement (binding) |
| **3** | Adjudication / Dispute Adjudication Board (DAB) | 60–90 days | Adjudicative, interim binding | DAB Decision (binding until challenged in arbitration) |
| **4** | Arbitration (final and binding) | 12–24 months | Adjudicative, final binding | Arbitral Award (final, enforceable) |

**Operational Logic:**
- Tier 1 is the *cheapest and fastest* gate. Most routine commercial disputes are settled here.
- Tier 2 introduces a *neutral facilitator* (mediator) who helps bridge communication gaps without imposing a decision.
- Tier 3 introduces a *quasi-binding adjudication* — the DAB's decision must be complied with immediately, preserving cash flow and project momentum, even if it is later challenged in arbitration.
- Tier 4 is the *final and binding* mechanism — the arbitral award is enforceable as a decree of the court under Section 36 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996.

**Best-Practice Additions:**
- A *duty to continue performance* clause ensuring that disputes do not halt project execution.
- A *no waiver* clause stating that failure to invoke a tier does not waive subsequent tiers.
- A *standstill* provision that no party may commence litigation or arbitration until all tiers have been exhausted.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — December 2024 Model Question — Module 4 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the various **Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR) methods** in detail. Compare and contrast mediation, conciliation, and arbitration. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is an **arbitration agreement**? Discuss the **essentials of a valid arbitration agreement** under Section 7 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996. Differentiate between an *arbitration clause* and a *submission agreement*. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Alternative Dispute Resolution Methods:**

ADR methods are processes that resolve disputes outside the traditional court system. They are categorized as follows:

1. **Consensual/Non-Binding Methods:**
   - **Negotiation:** Direct discussion between parties without third-party intervention. Lowest cost, fastest.
   - **Mediation:** A neutral third-party *mediator* facilitates communication and helps parties reach a voluntary settlement. Mediator has no authority to impose a decision. Governed by the **Mediation Act, 2023** in India.
   - **Conciliation:** Similar to mediation, but the *conciliator* plays a more active role — proposing solutions, suggesting terms, and actively working to bridge the gap. Governed by **Part III of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996** (Sections 61–81).
   - **Mini-Trial:** A structured settlement process where each party presents its case to senior executives (or a neutral adviser), who then negotiate a settlement.

2. **Consensual/Binding Method:**
   - **Arbitration:** A private adjudicative process where the arbitrator (or tribunal) issues a final, binding award. Governed by **Part I of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996** (Sections 2–43).

3. **Adjudicative/Quasi-Judicial Methods:**
   - **Dispute Adjudication Board (DAB):** A standing panel that issues interim binding decisions. Common in FIDIC contracts.
   - **Expert Determination:** A specialist (e.g., quantity surveyor, structural engineer) decides on a defined technical issue.

**Comparison Table: Mediation vs. Conciliation vs. Arbitration**

| Parameter | Mediation | Conciliation | Arbitration |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|-------------|
| **Nature** | Facilitative | Recommendatory (with active role) | Adjudicative |
| **Binding?** | No (settlement is binding if reached) | No (settlement is binding if reached) | Yes, final and binding |
| **Role of Neutral** | Facilitator | Active intervener | Adjudicator |
| **Governing Law** | Mediation Act, 2023 | Sections 61–81, A&C Act, 1996 | Sections 2–43, A&C Act, 1996 |
| **Enforceability of Award** | As a contract | As a contract | Section 36 (court decree) + 170+ countries (NY Convention) |
| **Court Intervention** | None | Minimal (Sections 77, 78) | Sections 9, 34, 36 |
| **Best Use Case** | Relationship preservation | Active problem-solving | Final resolution |

**Part (b) — Arbitration Agreement and its Essentials:**

An **arbitration agreement** is a written agreement by which parties to a contract agree to submit to arbitration all or certain disputes that have arisen or may arise between them in respect of a defined legal relationship.

**Essentials Under Section 7:**

1. **In Writing** — must be a document signed by the parties, or in an exchange of letters, telex, telegram, or electronic communication, or by incorporation by reference.
2. **Defined Legal Relationship** — the dispute must arise from a legal relationship, whether contractual or non-contractual.
3. **Parties' Capacity** — both parties must have legal capacity.
4. **Arbitrability** — the subject matter must be capable of being resolved by arbitration.
5. **Free Consent** — no fraud, coercion, or misrepresentation.
6. **Determinable Procedural Framework** — either specify or provide for the determination of the number of arbitrators, the procedure, and the seat.

**Difference Between Arbitration Clause and Submission Agreement:**

| Parameter | Arbitration Clause | Submission Agreement |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Timing** | Embedded in the main contract *before* dispute arises | Executed *after* the dispute has arisen |
| **Statutory Basis** | Section 7(2)(a), A&C Act, 1996 | Section 7(2)(b), A&C Act, 1996 |
| **Purpose** | Pre-emptive — anticipates future disputes | Reactive — addresses existing disputes |
| **Example** | "All disputes arising out of this contract shall be referred to arbitration." | "The parties hereby agree to refer their existing dispute regarding the defective pile foundation to arbitration." |
| **Legal Effect** | Triggers the entire arbitral process | Triggers arbitration for that specific dispute only |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 — "Conflicts, Disputes & Resolution"**
>
> 1. **Treating "conflict" and "dispute" as synonyms** — examiners WILL deduct 1–2 marks for this. Memorize the difference.
> 2. **Forgetting the statutory citations** — always cite *Section 7* for arbitration agreement, *Section 16* for kompetenz-kompetenz, *Section 29A* for the 12-month time limit, *Section 34* for setting aside, *Section 36* for enforcement. Examiners reward statutory accuracy heavily.
> 3. **Listing causes without categorization** — students often write 10 causes in a single paragraph. Examiners want *categorized* answers (Contractual / Behavioural / Project / External / Financial). Marks are awarded for structure.
> 4. **Drawing tiered clauses without time limits or legal nature** — the table is mandatory. A clause without duration is incomplete.
> 5. **Confusing "conciliation" with "mediation"** — these are distinct under Indian law. Mediation is governed by the *Mediation Act, 2023*; conciliation is governed by *Part III of the Arbitration & Conciliation Act, 1996*.
> 6. **Quoting international case law without Indian context** — always anchor your answer in *Indian* jurisprudence (e.g., *Enercon v. Enercon (India), 2014*; *Vodafone v. Union of India, 2012*).
> 7. **Failing to mention "separability"** — the doctrine that the arbitration agreement survives the main contract is a frequent Part A question.
> 8. **Forgetting the "Duty to Continue Performance"** — this is a best-practice element that examiners love testing in Part B.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision:**

#### A. Concepts (Must-Memorize)
- **Conflict** = latent, underlying, pre-dispute tension.
- **Dispute** = crystallized, formal, actionable claim.
- **Conflict Avoidance** = proactive mechanisms (partnering, risk registers, DAB, open-book accounting).
- **Tiered (MTDR) Clause** = sequential dispute resolution: Negotiation → Mediation → Adjudication → Arbitration.
- **ADR** = alternative to court litigation — includes negotiation, mediation, conciliation, arbitration, expert determination, mini-trial, DAB.
- **Arbitration Agreement** = written agreement under Section 7, A&C Act, 1996, may be a clause or a submission agreement.

#### B. Statutory Reference (Must-Cite)
- **Section 7** — Definition and form of arbitration agreement.
- **Section 9** — Interim measures by court.
- **Section 10** — Number of arbitrators (default = 1).
- **Section 16** — Kompetenz-kompetenz (tribunal rules on its own jurisdiction).
- **Section 29A** — 12-month time limit for arbitral award (extendable by 6 months).
- **Section 34** — Setting aside an arbitral award (90-day limitation).
- **Section 36** — Enforcement as court decree.
- **Mediation Act, 2023** — governs mediation in India.
- **Commercial Courts Act, 2015** — governs commercial litigation.

#### C. 4-Tier Clause (Must-Memorize)
- **Tier 1:** Negotiation (14–30 days).
- **Tier 2:** Mediation (30–60 days).
- **Tier 3:** Adjudication/DAB (60–90 days, interim binding).
- **Tier 4:** Arbitration (12–24 months, final binding).

#### D. Best Practices (10-Point Framework)
1. Use tiered clauses.
2. Choose the right ADR method.
3. Select qualified neutrals.
4. Maintain documentation.
5. Use FIDIC/NEC standard forms.
6. Prefer institutional arbitration.
7. Issue timely notices.
8. Preserve confidentiality.
9. Use early neutral evaluation.
10. Avoid hybrid clauses.

#### E. Common Pitfalls (Always Avoid)
- Equating conflict with dispute.
- Confusing mediation with conciliation.
- Forgetting statutory citations.
- Quoting figures as statutory thresholds.
- Mixing arbitration with court appeal.

#### F. Key Arbitrability Principle
Arbitration is *not available* for: criminal offences, matrimonial matters, guardianship, insolvency, testamentary matters, and matters involving public policy. **Memorize this list.**

#### G. Key Indian Case Law (Optional — for Higher Marks)
- *Enercon (India) v. Enercon GmbH, (2014) 5 SCC 1* — defining "seat" of arbitration.
- *Vodafone International Holdings BV v. Union of India, (2012) 6 SCC 613* — on arbitrability of tax disputes.
- *Renusagar Power Co. v. General Electric Co., AIR 1994 SC 860* — on public policy and enforcement of foreign awards.
- *SBP & Co. v. Patel Engineering Ltd., (2005) 8 SCC 618* — on the role of courts in appointing arbitrators.
- *BALCO v. Kaiser Aluminium Technical Services, (2012) 9 SCC 552* — on the doctrine of separability and kompetenz-kompetenz.

> **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — PECET863 CONTRACTS MANAGEMENT**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Composition of Arbitral Tribunal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET863 - CONTRACTS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Composition of Arbitral Tribunal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Arbitral Tribunal** is a quasi-judicial body constituted by one or more arbitrators, empowered by the written agreement of the disputing parties to adjudicate their commercial or civil disputes privately, outside the ambit of the conventional court system. The **Composition of the Arbitral Tribunal** refers to the entire legal and procedural mechanism governing *how* this tribunal is structured — i.e., the **number of arbitrators**, the **method of their appointment**, the **eligibility criteria** they must satisfy, the **disclosure norms** they must abide by, the **grounds on which they may be challenged**, and the **circumstances under which their mandate may be terminated**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Foundation:** In India, the entire framework for the composition of an Arbitral Tribunal is governed by **Sections 10 to 15 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996 (as amended by the Arbitration and Conciliation (Amendment) Act, 2015 and 2019)**. A KTU examiner expects you to cite sections precisely.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Private Court"

Imagine a scenario where two business partners, **A** and **B**, have a falling out over a joint venture agreement. Instead of going to the public district court (which is slow, expensive, and public), they decide in advance — at the time of signing their original contract — that if a dispute arises, they will hand it over to a **mutually respected neutral expert**, say a retired High Court judge or a chartered engineer. This expert acts as a **"private judge."**

- If they appoint **one** expert, he/she is called a **Sole Arbitrator**.
- If they appoint **three** experts (typically two chosen by the parties, who then pick a presiding umpire), it is a **Three-Member Tribunal**.
- This entire pre-agreed or post-dispute "assembly of private judges" is the **Arbitral Tribunal**, and the *rules of how this assembly is formed* is the **Composition of the Arbitral Tribunal**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter to a Civil Engineer?**
> In construction contracts (FIDIC, NEC, or Indian CPWD/PPP contracts), disputes over extension of time, variations, measurements, and delays are almost universally resolved by arbitration. The composition clause (Clause 20 of FIDIC Red Book 1999) determines *who* will decide your multi-crore claim. A poorly drafted arbitration clause often causes jurisdictional nightmares, leading to time and cost overruns.

## 1.3 Key Lexicon (Board-Standard Terminology)

| Term | Standard KTU Definition |
|---|---|
| **Arbitral Tribunal** | A single arbitrator or a panel of arbitrators constituted to adjudicate a dispute. |
| **Sole Arbitrator** | A tribunal consisting of a **single arbitrator** appointed to resolve the dispute. |
| **Umpire** | The presiding arbitrator in a three-member tribunal (the one who presides when there is no majority). |
| **Mandate** | The legal authority/duty of the arbitrator to perform the arbitration. |
| **Disclosure** | A written statement by the arbitrator revealing any past/present relationship that may raise doubts on independence/impartiality. |
| **Grounds for Challenge** | Statutory or contractual reasons due to which a party's nominee arbitrator may be disqualified. |
| **Grounds of Recusal** | Circumstances under which an arbitrator voluntarily withdraws from the case. |
| **De Jure Incapacitation** | Legal inability to function as an arbitrator (e.g., unsound mind, insolvency). |

## 1.4 The Five Governing Sections (Syllabus Highlight)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — High-Yield Sections to Memorize:**
> 1. **Section 10** — Number of Arbitrators
> 2. **Section 11** — Appointment of Arbitrators
> 3. **Section 12** — Grounds for Challenge
> 4. **Section 13** — Challenge Procedure
> 5. **Section 14** — Failure or impossibility to act
> 6. **Section 15** — Termination of mandate and substitution

The maximum depth any KTU question (even the 14-mark one) can extract is from these six sections. Master them, and the topic is locked.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Composition Lifecycle — Six Phased Logic Flow

The formation of an Arbitral Tribunal is **not a single event** but a **phased legal process**. Each phase is governed by a specific section of the Act. Understanding the "Why" behind each phase is critical for KTU answers.

### **Phase 1: Determination of the Number of Arbitrators (Section 10)**
- **The Default Rule:** The parties are free to determine the number of arbitrators. This is the principle of **Party Autonomy** — the cornerstone of arbitration law.
- **The Statutory Fallback:** Failing party agreement, the tribunal **shall consist of a Sole Arbitrator**.
- **Odd Number Mandate:** The Act implicitly favours an odd number to prevent deadlocks. However, parties *can* (rarely) agree to an even number with a default tie-breaking mechanism.

### **Phase 2: Appointment Procedure (Section 11)**
- **Party-Nominated Mechanism:** Parties are free to agree on a procedure. Typically, each party appoints one arbitrator, and the two appointees select a third who acts as the **Presiding Arbitrator/Umpire**.
- **High Court Appointing Authority (Post-2015 Amendment):** Where a party fails to act, or parties cannot agree, or the two appointed arbitrators cannot agree on the third within **30 days** from the date of their appointment, the appointment is made by the **High Court** (or its designated institution) upon application by either party.
  - *Why High Court and not Supreme Court?* — Because Section 11(6) was clarified by the 2015 Amendment to explicitly vest the power in the **High Court** to prevent the apex court from being flooded with Section 11 petitions.

### **Phase 3: Disclosure Norms (Fifth and Seventh Schedule + Section 12(1))**
- A prospective arbitrator is duty-bound to disclose, *before* acceptance, any circumstance likely to give rise to **justifiable doubts** about his/her independence or impartiality.
- The **Fifth Schedule** lists specific grounds (e.g., financial interest, prior legal representation, kinship with a party).
- A continuing obligation to disclose arises throughout the arbitration.

### **Phase 4: Grounds for Challenge (Section 12(3) & Fifth Schedule)**
- An arbitrator may be challenged only if:
  1. Circumstances exist that give rise to **justifiable doubts** as to his/her independence or impartiality (the **Subjective-Objective Test**).
  2. He/she **lacks the qualifications** agreed to by the parties.
- The **Seventh Schedule** (post-2015) makes these grounds *absolute* — a waiver of these grounds is void and the arbitrator is *automatically ineligible* (the *"automatic disqualification"* rule).

### **Phase 5: Challenge Procedure (Section 13)**
- A party intending to challenge must send a written statement of reasons to the tribunal within **15 days** of becoming aware of the constitution or the grounds.
- The first opportunity to raise a plea of ineligibility is in the *Statement of Claim and Defence*; failure to do so leads to waiver.
- The challenged arbitrator may withdraw; if not, the arbitral institution decides, failing which the High Court decides.

### **Phase 6: Termination and Substitution (Sections 14 & 15)**
- The mandate terminates if:
  - The arbitrator **becomes de jure or de facto unable** to perform (illness, death, refusal).
  - The arbitrator **withdraws from office** for any reason.
  - The parties agree to terminate.
- **Substitution Rule:** A substitute arbitrator **must** be appointed following the *same procedure* used for the original appointment. This preserves the *immutable nature of the appointment process*.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is the "formula sheet" equivalent for law subjects. Memorize the numbers and section references — they are the KTU equivalent of `F = ma`.**

| Provision (Section) | Statutory Rule | Standard Timeline / Number | KTU Mnemonic |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sec 10** — Number of Arbitrators | Parties free to decide; default = **Sole Arbitrator** | **One** (default) | *"Auto = 1"* |
| **Sec 11(3)** — Time to appoint | If no procedure agreed, the default rule applies | **30 days** for party to appoint | *"30 for one"* |
| **Sec 11(4)** — Default rule of 3-arbitrator panel | Each party appoints 1, the two appoint the 3rd (umpire) within **30 days** | **30 days** for the third | *"Two pick Three"* |
| **Sec 11(6)** — High Court appointment | On failure, High Court (not SC) appoints | No fixed timeline | *"HC is the Boss"* |
| **Sec 12(1)** — Disclosure | Prospective arbitrator must disclose conflicts | Before acceptance + ongoing | *"Tell all"* |
| **Sec 12(3)** — Grounds for Challenge | Independence, impartiality, agreed qualifications | — | *"Justifiable Doubts"* |
| **Sec 12(5) + Sch VII** — Ineligibility | Grounds in Sch VII cannot be waived | — | *"Auto-Disqualify"* |
| **Sec 13(2)** — Challenge time | Within **15 days** of becoming aware of grounds | **15 days** | *"15-day shield"* |
| **Sec 13(3)** — First opportunity rule | Ineligibility plea to be raised in Claim/Defence | — | *"First strike"* |
| **Sec 14(1)** — Termination of mandate | Inability to perform or withdrawal | — | *"Death or Distress"* |
| **Sec 15(1)** — Substitution | Same procedure as original appointment | — | *"Mirror Rule"* |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a real **FIDIC construction contract** (used by NHAI, DMRC, and international EPC projects):

- The **Engineer** (often the PMC) first attempts dispute resolution (Clause 20.1).
- Failing that, the dispute is referred to a **Dispute Adjudication Board (DAB)** — *functionally a mini-arbitral tribunal of 1 or 3 members*.
- If unsatisfied, the party escalates to arbitration under **ICC Rules** (typically a 3-member tribunal).
- The **composition clause** (often Clause 20.6 of FIDIC 1999) determines whether the DAB members become the arbitrators or fresh arbitrators are appointed.

> A construction engineer who understands this chain can advise clients on *drafting the arbitration clause at the tender stage* — a poorly drafted composition clause can render an award unenforceable under **Section 34** of the Act.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note to the student:** This is a legal/procedural topic, not a numerical engineering one. Hence, instead of algebraic derivations, this section presents the *legal-derivation of each statutory rule* with the same step-by-step rigour as a math derivation. The "formula" being derived is the *applicable legal position*.

## 3.1 Derivation I — Determining the Number of Arbitrators (Section 10)

**Step 1: Identify the Source of the Rule.**
The starting point is the principle of **Party Autonomy**, codified under Section 10(1) of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996.

**Step 2: Apply the Statutory Logic.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of Arbitrators (N)} & = \text{Number agreed by parties} \\
& \quad \text{[PRIMARY RULE — Section 10(1)]} \\
\text{If N is unagreed,} \quad N & = 1 \quad \text{[Sole Arbitrator — Section 10(2)]} \\
\text{If N is unagreed but parties desire 3,} \quad N & = 3 \quad \text{[Section 11(5) default]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Textual Conversion Logic.**
- If the contract states "any dispute shall be referred to arbitration" without specifying the number, the tribunal *automatically* becomes a **Sole Arbitrator Tribunal**.
- This is a *mandatory default*, not a discretionary one. The court has no power to order a 3-member tribunal if the parties have been silent on the number.

**Step 4: Exception — Even Number.**
The Act does not explicitly prohibit an even number, but Section 29(1) presumes a *majority decision*. Hence, even-numbered tribunals are practically non-existent in commercial contracts.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — The 30-Day Time-Slot Matrix (Section 11)

This is the most **numerically rigorous** aspect of the topic and is a frequent KTU question setter's favourite.

**Step 1: Define the Trigger Event.**
The clock starts on the date **a request for arbitration is received by the other party** or the date of *deemed* initiation per the agreed procedure.

**Step 2: Apply the Sequential Logic.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_0 & = \text{Date of Arbitration Notice} \\
T_1 & = T_0 + 30 \text{ days} \quad \text{[Deadline for Party A to appoint its arbitrator]} \\
T_2 & = T_1 + 30 \text{ days} \quad \text{[Deadline for Party B to appoint its arbitrator]} \\
T_3 & = T_2 + 30 \text{ days} \quad \text{[Deadline for the 2 appointees to agree on the 3rd/umpire]} \\
T_{\text{HC}} & = \text{Any time after } T_1 \text{ or } T_3 \text{ (whichever fails first)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Interpretation.**
- Total maximum period for a 3-member tribunal to be self-constituted: **90 days from the notice** (3 successive slots of 30 days each).
- If any slot lapses, the aggrieved party can approach the **High Court under Section 11(6)**.

**Step 4: Textual Logic — Why 30 days?**
The 30-day slots are *deemed fair periods* under commercial law. The 2015 Amendment reduced earlier ambiguities by enforcing strict timelines.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — The "Justifiable Doubts" Test (Section 12)

This is the *qualitative test* for challenging an arbitrator.

**Step 1: Identify the Test.** The test is a hybrid **Subjective + Objective** standard.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Justifiable Doubts (JD)} & = \text{Subjective Perception of the party} \\
& \quad + \text{Objective Reasonable Person Test}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the Test.**
- A *reasonable, fair-minded, and informed person* (the "objective bystander") must conclude that the circumstances would give rise to doubts about independence or impartiality.
- Mere suspicion is insufficient; there must be *justifiable* (i.e., legally supportable) doubt.

**Step 3: Statutory Reinforcement — Schedule VII.**
Post the 2015 Amendment, the **Seventh Schedule** lists specific ineligibilities (e.g., the arbitrator has a personal or business relationship with a party, or has previously advised one party). These are *absolute* — a person falling within Sch VII is *ipso facto* ineligible, and any waiver of this ineligibility is *null and void* (per Section 12(5)).

**Step 4: Practical Implication.**
An arbitrator who represented a party in *any* prior matter cannot later sit as arbitrator in a dispute involving that same party. The 2015 Amendment thus codified the *deemed bias* doctrine.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Substitution Algebra (Section 15)

**Step 1: Trigger Event.** The original arbitrator dies, becomes incapacitated, resigns, or is successfully challenged.

**Step 2: Apply the Rule.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Procedure for Substitute} & = \text{Procedure for Original Appointment} \\
& \quad \text{[Mirror Rule — Section 15(1)]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Consequence — Re-Start Rule.**
The substituted arbitrator *does not automatically* inherit the hearings already conducted. The arbitral tribunal is reconstituted and may, in its discretion, allow prior evidence to stand, but no party can *demand* this.

**Step 4: Textual Logic.**
This protects the legitimacy of the tribunal — the new arbitrator is chosen by the *same process*, ensuring that the party who nominated the prior arbitrator does not gain an unfair advantage from the substitution.

---

## 3.5 KTU 14-Mark Analytical Framework

When a KTU 14-mark question asks, *"Explain the composition of the Arbitral Tribunal under the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996"*, the examiner's valuation key typically expects the following **five-point structure**:

| S.No. | Component | Marks Allocated |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Definition + Statutory Framework (Sections 10–15) | 2 |
| 2 | Number of Arbitrators + Sole vs. Multi-Member | 3 |
| 3 | Appointment Procedure (Section 11 + HC jurisdiction) | 3 |
| 4 | Grounds for Challenge (Sections 12 & 13) | 3 |
| 5 | Termination & Substitution (Sections 14 & 15) | 2 |
| 6 | Citation of relevant case law (e.g., *ONGC v. Saw Pipes*) | 1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** The single mark for case law is often the "tiebreaker" between a 13/14 and 14/14. Always cite one case — e.g., **Perkins Eastman Architects v. HSCC (2019)** (on Section 11 appointment) or **SBP v. Patel Engineering (2005)** (on the role of courts in appointment).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Composition Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Contract Signed] --> B{Dispute Arises}
    B --> C[Arbitration Clause Invoked]
    C --> D{Section 10: Number Decided?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Proceed with agreed N]
    D -- No --> F[Sole Arbitrator Default]
    E --> G{Section 11: Appointment Procedure}
    F --> G
    G --> H[Parties Appoint Arbitrators]
    H --> I{Two Nominees Agree on 3rd?}
    I -- Yes --> J[3-Member Tribunal Formed]
    I -- No --> K[Section 11(6): High Court Steps In]
    K --> J
    J --> L{Section 12: Disclosure and Challenge}
    L -- Grounds Exist --> M[Section 13: Challenge Procedure]
    L -- Clean --> N[Tribunal Proceeds with Arbitration]
    M -- Sustained --> O[Section 15: Substitution]
    M -- Rejected --> N
    O --> N
    N --> P[Award Passed]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each box corresponds to a Section of the Arbitration Act. The flow is *strictly linear* — the tribunal *cannot* proceed to a Section 12 challenge without first being *constituted* under Section 10/11.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — The Appointment Decision Tree (Section 11)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Trigger: Request for Arbitration] --> P1{Parties Agreed on Procedure?}
    P1 -- Yes --> A1[Follow Contractual Procedure]
    P1 -- No --> P2{Section 11(5) Trigger}
    P2 -- 3 arbitrators requested --> P3[Each Party Appoints 1 within 30 days]
    P2 -- Otherwise --> P4[Sole Arbitrator]
    P3 --> P5{2nd Party Appoints within 30 days?}
    P5 -- Yes --> P6[2 Nominees Select 3rd Umpire]
    P5 -- No --> P7[Section 11(6): HC Appointing Authority]
    P6 --> P8{3rd Selected within 30 days?}
    P8 -- Yes --> P9[3-Member Tribunal Constituted]
    P8 -- No --> P7
    P7 --> P9
    A1 --> P9
    P4 --> P9
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Challenge Mechanism (Sections 12 & 13)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Grounds under Section 12 or Sch VII] --> C2{Are Grounds Absolute Sch VII?}
    C2 -- Yes --> C3[Automatic Ineligibility No Waiver Possible]
    C2 -- No --> C4[Justifiable Doubts Test]
    C4 --> C5[Send Written Challenge within 15 Days]
    C5 --> C6[Challenged Arbitrator Withdraws Voluntarily]
    C6 -- Withdraws --> C7[Section 15: Substitute Appointed]
    C6 -- Refuses --> C8[Arbitral Institution Decides]
    C8 --> C9{Falls within 30 Days?}
    C9 -- Yes --> C7
    C9 -- No --> C10[High Court Decides under Sec 13(5)]
    C3 --> C7
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Inter-Participant Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PARTIES[Disputing Parties]
        A1[Party A Claimant]
        A2[Party B Respondent]
    end
    subgraph TRIBUNAL[Arbitral Tribunal]
        T1[Arbitrator 1 - Nominated by A]
        T2[Arbitrator 2 - Nominated by B]
        T3[Arbitrator 3 - Umpire]
    end
    subgraph AUTHORITY[External Authority]
        E1[High Court under Sec 11 6]
        E2[Arbitral Institution ICC SIAC]
    end
    A1 --> T1
    A2 --> T2
    T1 --> T3
    T2 --> T3
    A1 -. Appointment Fails .-> E1
    A2 -. Appointment Fails .-> E1
    T1 -. Challenge Procedure .-> E2
    T2 -. Challenge Procedure .-> E2
    E1 -. Designate .-> T3
    E2 -. Decide Challenge .-> T1
    E2 -. Decide Challenge .-> T2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** The dashed lines represent *fallback/jurisdictional* flows. The solid lines represent *primary procedural* flows. Examiners often award a half-mark extra when students visually distinguish the *primary* and *contingent* relationships in a diagram.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Q: Define the term "Arbitral Tribunal" and state the default number of arbitrators under the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996.**

**Model Answer:**
An *Arbitral Tribunal* is a quasi-judicial body consisting of one or more arbitrators appointed to adjudicate a commercial dispute in place of a regular court, by virtue of a written arbitration agreement between the parties.

Under **Section 10** of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, the parties are free to determine the number of arbitrators. Where the parties fail to do so, the arbitral tribunal **shall consist of a sole arbitrator** [Section 10(2)]. Thus, the *default number is one*, unless expressly agreed otherwise.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Writing "1 to 3 arbitrators" as the default. The *statutory default* is strictly **one** (Sole Arbitrator). Multi-member tribunals require *express* party agreement or invocation of Section 11(5) defaults.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Briefly explain the disclosure obligation of an arbitrator under the Arbitration Act.**

**Model Answer:**
Under **Section 12(1)** of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, a prospective arbitrator is legally bound to disclose, *before* accepting appointment, any circumstances that may give rise to **justifiable doubts** about his/her independence or impartiality.

Furthermore, the obligation is **continuing** in nature — the arbitrator must disclose any such circumstance that arises *during* the arbitration.

The **Fifth Schedule** of the Act enumerates specific grounds that mandate disclosure (e.g., prior relationship with a party, financial interest, prior legal representation), and the **Seventh Schedule** (introduced by the 2015 Amendment) lists grounds of *automatic disqualification* that cannot be waived.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

> **(a) [7 Marks] Explain the procedure for the appointment of arbitrators under Section 11 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996. What is the role of the High Court under Section 11(6)?**

> **(b) [7 Marks] Discuss the grounds on which an arbitrator may be challenged under Section 12 and explain the challenge procedure under Section 13.**

---

**Model Answer to Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Party Autonomy (Section 11(1)–(3)):**
The parties to an arbitration agreement are at liberty to determine the procedure for the appointment of arbitrators. The principle of *freedom of contract* governs the appointment process.

**Step 2 — Default Procedure for a 3-Member Tribunal (Section 11(5)):**
Where the parties have agreed to a three-member tribunal without specifying the appointment procedure:
- Each party must appoint **one arbitrator** within **30 days** of receiving a request from the other party.
- The two appointed arbitrators must, within **30 days** of the last appointment, agree on the **third arbitrator**, who acts as the **presiding/umpire arbitrator**.

**Step 3 — Failure of Procedure:**
If any of the above conditions is not fulfilled, **any party** may request the **Chief Justice** of the **High Court** (within whose jurisdiction the arbitration is to be held) to appoint the arbitrator(s).

**Step 4 — Role of the High Court under Section 11(6):**
- The High Court (or its designated person/institution) shall appoint the arbitrator(s) *after* due enquiry.
- The appointment is to be made *on the basis of qualifications agreed by the parties* and after considering the *requirements of neutrality, expertise, and independence*.
- The decision of the High Court is **final and non-appealable** (per the *Perkins Eastman v. HSCC*, 2019 ruling of the Supreme Court).
- Notably, the **Supreme Court is NOT the appointing authority** post the 2015 Amendment — only the High Court has this power.

**Step 5 — Designated Institutions:**
The High Court may designate an arbitral institution (e.g., ICADR, DIAC) to make the appointment on its behalf, ensuring administrative efficiency.

---

**Model Answer to Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Statutory Source (Section 12):**
An arbitrator may be challenged **only on two grounds**:
1. The existence of circumstances giving rise to *justifiable doubts* as to his/her independence or impartiality.
2. Lack of qualifications expressly agreed by the parties.

**Step 2 — The "Justifiable Doubts" Test:**
This is a hybrid standard — both *subjective* (the challenging party's perception) and *objective* (the view of a reasonable, informed third party). A mere vague apprehension is insufficient.

**Step 3 — The Fifth and Seventh Schedules:**
- The **Fifth Schedule** lists specific grounds requiring disclosure (financial interest, prior representation, kinship, etc.).
- The **Seventh Schedule** (post-2015 Amendment) makes certain grounds *absolute and non-waivable*. A person falling under Sch VII is *ipso facto ineligible* to act as arbitrator.

**Step 4 — Challenge Procedure (Section 13):**
- A party must send a *written statement of challenge* to the arbitral tribunal within **15 days** of becoming aware of the constitution of the tribunal or any ground for challenge.
- The challenging plea must be raised at the **first available opportunity**, typically in the *Statement of Claim or Defence*. Failure to do so constitutes waiver.

**Step 5 — Adjudication of Challenge:**
- The challenged arbitrator may voluntarily withdraw.
- If not, the arbitral institution decides; failing this, the **High Court** decides under Section 13(5).
- If the challenge succeeds, a substitute arbitrator is appointed under Section 15 following the *same procedure* used for the original appointment.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **(a) [7 Marks] Discuss the provisions relating to the number of arbitrators and the manner of their appointment under the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996.**

> **(b) [7 Marks] Explain the circumstances under which the mandate of an arbitrator terminates. How is a substitute arbitrator appointed under Section 15?**

---

**Model Answer to Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Number of Arbitrators (Section 10):**
The parties are free to determine the number of arbitrators. Where no number is fixed, the statutory default is a **Sole Arbitrator** (Section 10(2)). This default reflects the *judicial preference for speed, economy, and simplicity*.

**Step 2 — Statutory Preference for Sole Arbitrator:**
The Act reflects an international trend (UNCITRAL Model Law) favouring a sole arbitrator in most commercial disputes. A 3-member tribunal is more common in **high-value construction, infrastructure, and international EPC contracts** where the stakes justify higher costs.

**Step 3 — Manner of Appointment (Section 11):**
- **Where parties have agreed on a procedure:** That procedure prevails (party autonomy).
- **Where no procedure is agreed, but the tribunal is to consist of three arbitrators** (Section 11(5)):
  - Each party nominates one arbitrator within **30 days** of receipt of a request.
  - The two arbitrators jointly appoint the third arbitrator (presiding umpire) within **30 days**.
- **Where no procedure is agreed and the tribunal is to consist of a sole arbitrator** (Section 11(4)):
  - If the parties cannot agree, either party may approach the High Court under Section 11(6).

**Step 4 — The "Automatic Appointment" Rule (Section 11(6) — Post 2015):**
Where a party fails to act or the parties/two arbitrators cannot agree, *any party* may request the **Chief Justice of the appropriate High Court** to appoint the arbitrator. The High Court (or its designated institution) shall appoint after considering the agreed qualifications, if any.

**Step 5 — Finality and Non-Appealability:**
The decision of the High Court on an appointment is *final* and not subject to appeal. This was settled in *Perkins Eastman Architects v. HSCC (India) Ltd.* (2019) and clarified further in *In Re: Interplay between Arbitration Agreements and Stamp Duty (2023)*.

---

**Model Answer to Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Grounds for Termination of Mandate (Section 14):**
An arbitrator's mandate terminates when:
1. He/she **becomes de jure unable** to perform the functions (e.g., declared of unsound mind, adjudged insolvent, convicted of a criminal offence involving moral turpitude).
2. He/she **becomes de facto unable** to perform (e.g., prolonged illness, unexplained absence).
3. He/she **withdraws from office** for any reason (voluntary recusal).
4. The **parties agree** to terminate the mandate.

**Step 2 — The Concept of De Jure vs. De Facto Inability:**
- **De Jure:** A legal disability imposed by law (e.g., the arbitrator is now a serving judge).
- **De Facto:** A practical inability despite the absence of a legal bar (e.g., severe illness).

**Step 3 — Substitution Procedure (Section 15(1)):**
Where the mandate of an arbitrator terminates, a **substitute arbitrator** shall be appointed *following the same procedure* applicable to the original appointment. This is the *Mirror Rule* — the new arbitrator inherits the *legitimacy* of the original appointment.

**Step 4 — Re-Constitution of the Tribunal:**
The substitute arbitrator is appointed by the same appointing authority (parties, two arbitrators, or the High Court). The newly constituted tribunal *may*, in its discretion, allow prior evidence and proceedings to stand, but no party has a *vested right* to demand this.

**Step 5 — Importance of the Mirror Rule:**
- Prevents *arbitral corruption* by ensuring the substitute does not "inherit" the position without re-evaluation.
- Ensures that the party who originally nominated the arbitrator is *not prejudiced* by the substitution.
- Preserves the *fairness doctrine* embedded in Sections 12 and 13.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "appoint" and "constitute":** The tribunal is *constituted* when all arbitrators accept; merely *appointing* an arbitrator does not constitute the tribunal.
> 2. **Wrong Court:** Writing "Supreme Court" for Section 11(6) appointment. Post-2015, it is **strictly the High Court**. The SC's power was *struck down* in *SBP & Co. v. Patel Engineering* (2005) and replaced by the High Court in the Amendment.
> 3. **Ignoring Schedule VII:** Many students cite only Section 12(3) and miss the *absolute disqualification* under Schedule VII. This is a 2-mark deduction in typical 14-mark answers.
> 4. **Mixing the 15-day and 30-day time limits:** Section 13(2) challenge window is **15 days**; Section 11(3) appointment window is **30 days**. Confusing these is a classic KTU trap.
> 5. **Omitting case law:** A 14-mark answer without *at least one* case citation (e.g., *Perkins Eastman*, *ONGC v. Saw Pipes*, *Dharamveer v. Raj Singh*) loses the case-law mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Save This for the Night Before the Exam):**

- **Section 10:** Number of arbitrators — default = **1 (Sole Arbitrator)**; parties free to fix any number.
- **Section 11:** Appointment — party autonomy prevails; **High Court** is the ultimate appointing authority.
- **Section 11(5):** Default 3-member procedure: each party appoints 1; they jointly appoint the umpire; all within **30-day slots**.
- **Section 12(1):** Disclosure obligation — *before* acceptance and *ongoing*.
- **Section 12(3):** Grounds for challenge = *justifiable doubts* on independence/impartiality OR lack of agreed qualifications.
- **Section 12(5) + Sch VII:** *Absolute disqualification* — non-waivable. Ignorance is no defence.
- **Section 13(2):** Challenge window = **15 days** from becoming aware; first opportunity rule in pleadings.
- **Section 14:** Termination = de jure inability, de facto inability, withdrawal, or party agreement.
- **Section 15:** Substitution = *same procedure* as original (Mirror Rule). Re-constitution may entail repeat hearings.
- **Key Case Law (must-cite):** *SBP v. Patel Engineering* (2005), *ONGC v. Saw Pipes* (2003), *Perkins Eastman v. HSCC* (2019), *Boomerang v. IFFCO* (2017), *HRD Corp v. GAIL* (2018).
- **Key Mnemonic:** *"10-15-30" — 10 defines number, 15 days for challenge, 30 days for appointment slot."*
- **Trap:** Even-numbered tribunals are practically non-existent and not the statutory default.
- **Construction Engineering Link:** FIDIC Clause 20.6 / NEC Option W2 are *real-world* embodiments of Section 10–15 of the Indian Act. KTU loves this interdisciplinary linkage.

> **End of Notes — Module 4 / Composition of Arbitral Tribunal**
